body_txt: A character who walks into a scene, says a few lines at most, and departs . Essentially, an extra with a line, but it's a speaking role, usually enough to warrant membership in one of the actors' guilds, and some of these characters are One Scene Wonders , earning great acclaim despite their minimal screentime. Plus, a part this small wouldn't be cast unless it served a purpose in the story , however small... right? The term comes from Greek theater, specifically tragedy: While the plot of a given play focused on members of important nobility, the theater still needed actors to fill the role of their military entourage — you know, the kind of soldiers whose only job on the stage is to stand at attention and give a salute whenever a noble walks by them. It has also become the standard term for an operatic supernumerary, one of several silent extras (of either sex) who may carry anything from a flagon to a kidnap victim. See here. In mid-20th century domestic dramas, this was often the role of household servant characters, who would be neither seen nor heard at most times, but occasionally would be called on to deliver some tidbit of dialogue that none of the main characters could credibly utter. In modern TV & film production (at least those under SAG-AFTRA purview) such roles are called "Under-fives", and specifically refer to a role with more time onscreen than an Extra but less than a Day Player, speaking no more than five lines of dialogue or fifty words. A Bit Character or Flat Character are the next step up. Compare with an Almost Dead Guy , who walks into a scene, delivers a message, and dies horribly, a Herald , who's usually more dramatic and foreshadows The Call , a Red Shirt , a Pursued Protagonist , and The Cameo . For a generally dull character, see The Generic Guy (or Girl ). Mandatory Line applies when this role is played by an established character. Not to be confused with the native assistant of the Great White Hunter , with The Lancer , or with literal wielders of spears.
